result.markdown:
--- title: "Get started" subtitle: "From event timing to a typed first-level fit." jupyter: python3 --- ## Install ```bash pip install fmrimod ``` For local development from this checkout: ```bash pip install -e . ``` ## The 30-Second Tour Start with an event table and bind it to data in an `fmri_dataset`. The event table stores experimental timing; the dataset keeps data, TR, and events together. ::: {#698f57e2 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} import numpy as np import pandas as pd import fmrimod as fm from fmrimod.spec import hrf from fmrimod.contrast import condition events = pd.DataFrame({ "onset": np.arange(8, 210, 12.0), "condition": np.where(np.arange(17) % 2 == 0, "face", "scene"), "duration": 2.0, }) ``` ::: The design is a **typed value**, built with `hrf(...)`. You can read its fields before anything is fitted — it is not an opaque string. ::: {#a4f3abda .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} spec = hrf("condition", basis="spmg1") spec.variables, spec.hrf ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ``` (('condition',), 'spmg1') ``` ::: ::: So the result can be *checked*, not just shape-inspected, we simulate data with a **known** face−scene effect of `2.0` in five of ten voxels. The signal is placed by the *typed* column identity — we ask the model which column is `term="condition", level="face"`, never hard-code a column name. ::: {#e9d08350 .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} rng = np.random.default_rng(7) probe = fm.fmri_lm(spec, fm.fmri_dataset(np.zeros((220, 10)), tr=1.0, events=events)) face_ix = next(c.index for c in probe.model.design_columns() if c.term == "condition" and c.level == "face") X = np.asarray(probe.model.design_matrix_array(run=0)) true_beta = np.zeros((X.shape[1], 10)) true_beta[face_ix, :5] = 2.0 Y = X @ true_beta + rng.normal(scale=1.5, size=(220, 10)) dataset = fm.fmri_dataset(Y, tr=1.0, events=events) ``` ::: Fit through the canonical first-level seam, then state the hypothesis by **naming conditions** — not column positions. The recovered estimate returns the injected effect. ::: {#f5ed7430 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} fit = fm.fmri_lm(spec, dataset) faces = fit.contrast( condition("face", term="condition") - condition("scene", term="condition"), name="face_minus_scene", ) pd.DataFrame({ "estimate": faces.estimate.round(3), "t": faces.stat.round(2), "p": faces.p_value.round(4), }) # voxels 0–4 recover ≈ 2.0; voxels 5–9 are null ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ```{=html}
estimate t p
0 2.034 74.59 0.0000
1 2.038 67.70 0.0000
2 2.049 68.47 0.0000
3 1.943 66.67 0.0000
4 1.999 69.35 0.0000
5 0.021 0.66 0.5104
6 -0.043 -1.40 0.1633
7 0.042 1.40 0.1615
8 0.009 0.29 0.7747
9 -0.007 -0.24 0.8124
``` ::: ::: The realized design is readable from the fit — built for you, still inspectable. ::: {#f56c80ef .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} list(fit.model.design_matrix(run=0).columns) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=5} ``` ['condition_condition.face', 'condition_condition.scene', 'base_constant1_block_1'] ``` ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true"} ## Coming from R / `fmrireg`? The R-style string formula still works and compiles to the *same* spec: `fm.fmri_lm("hrf(condition)", dataset)` is exact sugar for `fm.fmri_lm(hrf("condition"), dataset)`. Prefer the typed form in Python — it is composable with `+`, IDE-introspectable, and inspectable before fitting. ::: ## What Just Happened `fmri_dataset()` binds data to timing and events, `hrf(...)` is the typed design spec, `fmri_lm()` fits the public first-level path, and `fit.contrast(...)` states the hypothesis by name. This is the same spine the flagship parity workflows use: ```text fmri_dataset -> fmri_lm(spec) -> contrast -> group ``` The **[golden path](tutorials/golden-path.qmd)** walks this spine continuously — first level *into* group — and checks the recovered numbers against the R/Nilearn reference on the page. ## Next Steps - [Tutorial: the golden path](tutorials/golden-path.qmd) — the full spine, end to end - [Tutorial: overview and workflow](tutorials/overview-workflow.qmd) - [Tutorial: design matrices](tutorials/design-matrices.qmd) - [Tutorial: linear model](tutorials/linear-model.qmd) - [Tutorial: contrasts](tutorials/contrasts.qmd) - [API reference](reference/index.qmd)
